name: Thayir Sadam (Curd Rice)
description: A cooling Tamil Nadu staple of soft rice mashed with creamy yogurt, finished with a mustard-curry leaf tadka. Eaten at the end of every South Indian meal as a digestive.
instructions: Mash the cooked rice while warm with the back of a ladle until slightly pasty.
Mix in the curd and milk; stir until smooth and creamy. Season with salt.
Heat ghee in a small pan and splutter mustard seeds.
Add curry leaves, green chilli, and grated ginger; sauté 30 seconds.
Pour the tadka over the curd rice and mix gently.
Garnish with pomegranate seeds and serve at room temperature or chilled.
tips: Use slightly warm rice for curd to absorb better. Overcooked rice gives the best texture.

name: Appam (Kerala Rice Hoppers)
description: Lacy, bowl-shaped rice crepes with crispy edges and a soft, pillowy center — the iconic Kerala breakfast, traditionally served with vegetable stew or coconut milk.
instructions: Blend soaked raw rice, cooked rice, fresh coconut, and coconut water into a smooth batter.
Dissolve yeast and sugar in 2 tbsp warm water; add to batter. Rest 6–8 hours until fermented.
Stir in salt just before cooking.
Heat a seasoned appam chatti (or non-stick pan) over medium heat.
Pour a ladle of batter in the centre, swirl the pan to spread thin lacy edges.
Cover and cook 2 minutes until edges are crispy and centre is set.
Serve immediately with coconut milk sweetened with sugar.
tips: Fermentation time is key — longer in cold climates. Don't skip swirling the pan immediately after pouring.

name: Kerala Chemmeen Curry (Prawn Curry)
description: Fiery and tangy Kerala prawn curry cooked in a coconut milk gravy with Kudampuli (Gambooge) — the trademark souring agent of Keralan seafood cooking.
instructions: Soak Kudampuli in 1/2 cup warm water for 10 minutes.
Heat coconut oil in a clay pot; splutter mustard seeds and curry leaves.
Sauté shallots, ginger, and garlic until golden. Add green chillies.
Add red chilli powder, turmeric, and coriander powder; cook the masala 2 minutes.
Pour in thin coconut milk and Kudampuli with its soaking water. Bring to a simmer.
Add prawns and cook 5–7 minutes until just pink.
Stir in thick coconut milk. Heat without boiling, adjust salt, and serve over rice.
tips: Using a clay pot enhances the earthy flavour. Never boil after adding thick coconut milk.

name: Puttu with Kadala Curry
description: Cylindrical steamed rice-and-coconut cakes served alongside a rich black chickpea curry — one of Kerala's most beloved breakfast combinations.
instructions: Pressure cook soaked chickpeas until soft (3 whistles). Reserve the cooking water.
Heat oil, splutter mustard seeds and curry leaves. Add onions and sauté until golden.
Add ginger garlic paste, then tomatoes. Cook until oil separates.
Add all spices, stir, then add chickpeas and cooking water. Simmer 10 minutes until thick.
For puttu: mix puttu flour with salt and just enough water to make a crumbly, moist texture — not a dough.
Layer puttu mould alternately with flour mix and grated coconut. Steam 5–8 minutes.
Unmould the cylindrical puttu and serve hot alongside kadala curry.
tips: The puttu flour texture is ready when a fistful holds shape but crumbles. Don't make it wet.

name: Semiya Payasam (Vermicelli Pudding)
description: A fragrant, golden South Indian dessert of roasted vermicelli slow-cooked in full-fat milk with cardamom, saffron, and crunchy cashews — a must at every festival.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: a pinch, soaked in 2 tbsp warm milk

instructions: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pan; fry cashews until golden and raisins until plump. Set aside.
In the same ghee, roast vermicelli until fragrant and light golden.
Pour in the milk and bring to a boil, stirring frequently.
Add cardamom; simmer 15 minutes, stirring, until vermicelli is completely soft.
Add sugar, condensed milk, and saffron milk. Simmer 5 more minutes until thickened.
Stir in rose water. Garnish with fried cashews and raisins. Serve warm or chilled.
tips: Payasam thickens considerably as it cools — add a splash of milk before reheating.

name: Murukku (Crispy Rice Spiral Snack)
description: Crunchy, sesame-flecked spiral snacks made from rice flour and urad dal flour — the quintessential Tamil Nadu Diwali treat that disappears from tins faster than they're made.

ingredient_quantities.water: as needed to make soft dough

instructions: Mix rice flour, urad dal flour, sesame seeds, cumin, asafoetida, and salt.
Rub in butter until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Gradually add water to form a soft, pliable dough (not sticky).
Fill a murukku press with the star-shaped disc. Press spirals onto a damp cloth.
Heat oil to 170°C. Slide murukku spirals into the oil carefully.
Fry 3–4 minutes on each side until golden and crisp. Drain on paper towels.
Cool completely before storing in an airtight tin.
tips: Test oil temperature with a small piece of dough — it should rise slowly and steadily.

name: Karimeen Pollichathu (Pearl Spot Fish in Banana Leaf)
description: The gem of Kerala's backwaters — Pearl Spot fish marinated in a spicy coconut masala, wrapped in banana leaves, and pan-roasted for a smoky, fragrant finish.
instructions: Score the fish on both sides and make slits to absorb marinade.
Grind shallots, coconut, green chilli, ginger, garlic into a paste. Mix with red chilli, turmeric, pepper, salt, and lemon juice.
Coat the fish thoroughly with the marinade inside slits and all over. Rest 20 minutes.
Heat coconut oil in a pan; fry shallots and curry leaves until golden. Set aside.
Wilt banana leaves briefly over a flame until pliable.
Place fish on a banana leaf, top with the fried shallot mixture, wrap tightly.
Cook the parcel in a hot pan with a little coconut oil, 8 minutes each side, until the leaf chars at edges. Serve in the leaf.
tips: Charring the banana leaf edges seals in steam and imparts an earthy, smoky flavour — don't rush this step.

name: Neer Dosa (Water Dosa)
description: Impossibly thin, snow-white, lace-like rice crepes from the Karnataka coast — made from just soaked rice and water, served with coconut chutney or chicken sukka.
instructions: Drain soaked rice; grind with 2 cups water until very smooth (thinner than regular dosa batter).
Add remaining water and salt — batter should be watery like thin buttermilk. No fermentation needed.
Heat a cast-iron pan over high heat. Grease lightly with coconut oil.
Reduce heat to medium. Pour a ladleful of batter from the edge inward in a circular motion — do not spread.
Immediately cover the pan. Cook 1–2 minutes; the dosa is ready when it lifts easily.
Do not flip — neer dosa is cooked only on one side.
Fold into quarters and serve hot with fresh coconut chutney.
tips: The batter must be very thin and the pan must be hot. Don't attempt to spread the batter.

name: Kori Gassi (Mangalorean Chicken Curry)
description: The soul of Tulu Nadu — a bold, deep-red Bunt-style chicken curry with a roasted coconut-spice paste, balanced with tangy Kudampuli and finished in fragrant coconut oil.
instructions: Dry-roast coriander seeds, cumin, peppercorns, and dried red chillies until fragrant. Cool.
Blend roasted spices, dry coconut, garlic, ginger, and turmeric with a little water into a smooth paste.
Soak Kudampuli in 1/2 cup warm water.
Heat coconut oil; fry onions until deep golden. Add curry leaves.
Add the ground masala paste; fry on medium heat until oil separates — about 8 minutes.
Add chicken pieces; coat well and cook on high heat 5 minutes.
Add Kudampuli with water, 1.5 cups water, and salt. Simmer covered 25 minutes.
Uncover and cook 5 more minutes to thicken. Finish with a drizzle of raw coconut oil.
tips: Use Byadagi chillies for colour without excessive heat; Guntur for heat. Roasting the coconut is non-negotiable for the authentic flavour.

name: Pesarattu (Moong Dal Dosa)
description: Andhra Pradesh's wholesome breakfast crepe made from whole green moong dal — no fermentation needed, crispy, protein-rich, and served with ginger chutney and upma stuffing.
instructions: Drain soaked moong dal and rice. Blend with green chilli, ginger, cumin, salt, and a little water into a smooth batter.
Stir in chopped coriander. Batter should be slightly thick but pourable.
Heat a non-stick pan over high heat. Pour a ladleful and spread in concentric circles.
Drizzle oil on edges. Scatter chopped onions on top.
Cook until edges crisp and bottom is golden, 2–3 minutes.
Flip briefly (30 seconds) or fold without flipping. Serve hot with ginger chutney.
tips: No fermentation needed — pesarattu batter can be used immediately. Whole moong gives the characteristic green colour.
